splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on the timing requirements for actuarial assumption selection in multiemployer pension plan withdrawal liability calculations. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in such plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of updated information in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be finalized by the end of the prior plan year (the statutory measurement date), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuit courts split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the supervised release term is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the period during which violations can be found.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that the supervised release term is not tolled during absconding, and courts lack authority to extend punishment beyond the statutory term without explicit congressional authorization.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress intended when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any previously presented claim, in addition to Section 2255(h) requirements. If not, they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional law rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners under § 2255, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The split implicates whether candidates must show concrete injury from ballot-counting rules or whether candidacy alone confers standing, affecting the availability of pre-election challenges to mail-in voting and ballot counting deadlines nationwide.

positions.label: Standing exists for candidates
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly dilute their votes or alter the competitive landscape, even before an election takes place.

positions.label: No standing for candidates
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election because they cannot demonstrate a concrete, particularized injury traceable to the challenged law.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that expand criminal liability.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the rule of lenity should take priority over agency (Sentencing Commission) deference when interpreting ambiguous sentencing guidelines provisions in the criminal context. The Third Circuit held that deference trumps lenity, while at least the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails, with the Sixth Circuit also expressing agreement in concurrence.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when guideline provisions are ambiguous, preventing agencies from using delegated power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of ambiguous guidelines even in the criminal context, applying Auer/Kisor deference before resorting to the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision. This determines whether a defendant must meet all three listed criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief or whether meeting any single criterion is disqualifying, with significant sentencing consequences.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is disqualifying, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' Application Note 1 to § 4B1.2, which extends the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, is a valid and authoritative interpretation of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' for the career-offender guideline is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor, given that the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses. This affects whether prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career-offender predicates.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of the career-offender guideline entitled to Stinson/Kisor deference, and thus conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses under § 4B1.2(b).

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text supports, thus is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits prohibit any reliance on such changes, while others allow them alone or in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement amendment attempted to allow this, but some circuits hold the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, as this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make those changes retroactive.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order in a criminal case is governed by the civil 30-day deadline or the criminal 14-day deadline under the Federal Rules of Appellate Procedure.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, noting a lopsided circuit split on this question.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (30 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal proceedings, so the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines even if the substance is not controlled under federal law (the Controlled Substances Act).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance underlying a state conviction to also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act. This matters particularly for state marijuana convictions in jurisdictions with broader scheduling than federal law.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — state conviction qualifies
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes 'an offense under state law' and there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the career offender guideline, so state drug convictions qualify regardless of federal scheduling.

positions.label: Federal-law limitation applies — substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that to qualify as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines, the substance underlying the state conviction must also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation inures to the estate, creating a circuit split as noted in the dissent.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with § 541(a)'s broad scope, means that post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate, to encourage good-faith conversion rather than penalizing it.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions about the conditions or terms of their confinement (such as denial of earned time credits or transfer to prerelease custody under the First Step Act) must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring civil rights claims.
splits.description: A longstanding and unresolved circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for challenges to conditions of confinement or whether prisoners may also use civil rights actions (Bivens or § 1983). This affects how federal prisoners can challenge BOP decisions regarding First Step Act earned time credits and prerelease custody transfers.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-related challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the fact or duration of confinement, including denial of earned time credits or prerelease custody, must be brought through habeas corpus and not through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights claims also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims (§ 1983 or Bivens) to challenge conditions of confinement, even when habeas relief might also be available, particularly when the challenge does not seek release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release at revocation, or whether revocation sentencing must serve only the purposes identified in 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e).
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, placing it at what internal dissenters call 'the extreme of a circuit split.' Other circuits hold that revocation sentencing should not serve purely punitive purposes but rather the limited purposes Congress identified in the supervised release statutes.

positions.label: Punishment permitted at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when imposing sentences upon revocation.

positions.label: Punishment not a proper purpose at revocation
positions.summary: Holds that revocation of supervised release is not meant to punish the violation itself, but rather to vindicate the court's authority and protect the public, consistent with the limited purposes Congress identified.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable as a collateral order because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be irreparably lost if review were delayed until final judgment. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of collateral orders, creating potential disagreement on the scope of the collateral order doctrine in the sealing context.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal documents are interlocutory and not immediately appealable, as the privacy interest can be adequately vindicated on appeal from final judgment or through other procedural mechanisms.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or similar federal law?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards for discrimination claims in hospitality settings, suggesting a circuit split on the applicable standards for liability and damages in such cases. The decision may resolve disagreements over proof requirements and available remedies.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied standards that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to warrant review in the context of discrimination claims against hospitality businesses.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishment contexts, contributing to the circuit split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
